---
title: "Title"
author:
    name: Dr Dan Chalk, Sammi Rosser
    affiliation: Health Service Modelling Associates Programme
execute:
  eval: false # Don't run code by default
  echo: true # Display code cells by default
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: false # necessary for stlite-quarto and chalkboard to work
    # embed-resources: true # comment in/out as appropriate
    chalkboard: true
    output-file: index.html # ensures picked up by github hosting
    view-distance: 100
    height: 800 # default height and width ensure consistent behaviour on different screens
    width: 1250 # would recommend leaving this and height value as they are
    preview-links: true
    controls: false
    controls-layout: edges
    transition: fade
    plotly-connected: true
    background-transition: fade
    mermaid:
      theme: neutral
      format: svg
    menu:
        numbers: true
    preload-iframes: true
    logo: "assets/hsma_logo.png"
    footer-logo-link: "https://hsma.co.uk"
    # background-image: "assets/hsma_bg_1.png"
    background-image: "assets/banner_hq.png"
    # background-size: cover
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    title-slide-attributes:
        data-background-image: assets/banner.png
    verticator:
      themetag: 'h1'
      color: ''
      inversecolor: ''
      skipuncounted: false
      clickable: true
      position: 'auto'
      offset: '3vmin'
      autogenerate: true
      tooltip: false
      scale: 1
    include-in-header:
      file: resources/header.html
revealjs-plugins:
  - verticator
  - fullscreen
  - pointer
  - codefocus
filters:
  - stlite # modified stlite extension
  - reveal-header
  - bergam0t/value-box
  - bergam0t/focus-list
  - code-fullscreen

---

# Section Header Example

Your Content Here

## Slide Example

This is a slide with some text on it.

# Code Examples

## Defining code snippets in the qmd file

```{python}
import plotly.express as px

px.scatter(x=[1,2,3], y=[2,3,4])
```


## Defining and running code snippets in the qmd file

```{python}
#| eval: true

import plotly.express as px
import plotly.io as pio
pio.renderers.default = "notebook"

px.scatter(x=[1,2,3], y=[2,3,4], height=400)
```

## Including just the output

```{python}
#| eval: true
#| echo: false

import plotly.express as px
import plotly.io as pio
pio.renderers.default = "notebook"

px.scatter(x=[1,2,3], y=[2,3,4])
```

## Importing code snippets from files

```{python}
{{< include code_examples/code_example_1.py >}}
```

## Importing and running code snippets

```{python}
#| eval: true
{{< include code_examples/code_example_1.py >}}
```

## Embedded Streamlit

```{stlite-python}
import micropip
await micropip.install("plotly")

import streamlit as st
from plotly import express as px

st.title("Sample App")

st.slider("Choose a number between 1 and 10", min_value=1, max_value=10, value=5)

st.plotly_chart(
    px.scatter(x=[1,2,3], y=[2,3,4])
)
```

# Layouts

## 2 col layout example

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width='50%'}

Here is column 1

:::

::: {.column width='50%'}

Here is column 2

:::

::::

## Incremental list reveal

This will be on the screen at the start.

:::{.incremental}

- Here's bullet point 1
- Here's bullet point 2

:::


## Fragments

This will be on the screen at the start.

:::{.fragment}

This won't appear until you press a button.

:::

:::{.fragment}

Then this will appear when you're ready.

![](assets/hsma_logo.png)

:::

## Simple boxes 1

::: {.value-box-simple .bg-amber style="font-size: 1.25rem;"}

**Value boxes**

A simple value box can help convey points, especially when combined with fragments.

:::

## Complex icon box layouts with index parameter

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Left Column
::: {.value-box fragment="true" index="2"}
This appears SECOND (Index 2)
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Right Column
::: {.value-box fragment="true" index="1"}
This appears FIRST (Index 1)
:::
:::

::::




# Other features

## Speaker notes

There are some speaker notes on this slide - but you can't see them unless you're in speaker view!

:::{.notes}
Aha! I'm secret notes.
:::


## No box {.no-box}

Use the {.no-box} syntax next to your slide title to remove the background grey box.

This only works on second level slides - not # level slides.


## Simple boxes 2

::: {.fragment .fade-in-then-semi-out}

::: {.value-box-simple .bg-light-blue style="font-size: 1.25rem;"}

**Value boxes**

A simple value box can help convey points, especially when combined with fragments.

:::

:::

::: {.fragment}

::: {.value-box-simple .bg-amber style="font-size: 1.25rem;"}

**Value boxes**

Like this!

:::


:::




## Icon boxes

::: {.value-box icon="bi-arrow-down-up" color="bg-light-blue" width="60%" align="center"}
And here's a more advanced type of box with an icon
:::

::: {.value-box value="Big text" icon="bi-arrow-down-up" color="bg-light-blue" width="60%" align="center" fragment="fade-in-then-out"}
And here's a more advanced type of box with an icon and a value
:::

::: {.value-box fragment="strike" width="60%" align="center"}
And one without an icon
:::


## Complex icon box layouts with index parameter

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Left Column
::: {.value-box fragment="true" index="2"}
This appears SECOND (Index 2)
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Right Column
::: {.value-box fragment="true" index="1"}
This appears FIRST (Index 1)
:::
:::

::::


## Fade-in-then-out-lists

You can use the {.focus-list} div to auto fade in and out as you move through a list

::: {.focus-list}
* First important point
* Second important point (previous one dims!)
* Third important point
:::


## Fade-in-then-out-lists

This works with indented lists too

::: {.focus-list}
* First important point
  * And a sub-point on that matter
* Second important point (previous one dims!)
  * And a sub-point on that matter
    * ... and a really nested point
* Third important point
:::




## Code focus

Using reunings code focus extension, we can highlight different parts of a code example.

```{python}
#| eval: false
answer = "42"
question = "What is the answer to life, the universe, and everything?"

print(f"Question: '{question}'\n Answer: '{answer}'")
```

::: {.fragment .current-only data-code-focus="1"}
When this fragment is shown, the first line of code will have the `focus` class added to it and it gets highlighted.
:::

::: {.fragment .current-only data-code-focus="3"}
Then this line when we move through
:::
